subject: Physics
q: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is $2.2$ times the energy required to remove an electron from Helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is:
question: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is $2.2$ times the energy required to remove an electron from Helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is:
solution:
Energy required to remove $\mathrm{e}^{-}$from singly ionized helium atom $=\frac{(13.6) Z^2}{1^2}=54.4 \mathrm{eV}$ $(\because \mathrm{Z}=2)$ Energy required to remove $\mathrm{e}^{-}$from helium atom $=x \mathrm{eV}$
According to question, $54.4 \mathrm{eV}=2.2 \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}$ $=24.73 \mathrm{eV}$
Therefore, energy required to ionize helium atom $=(54.4+24.73) \mathrm{eV}=79.12 \mathrm{eV}$

subject: Physics
q: The acceleration of an electron in the first orbit of the hydrogen atom (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>) is :
question: The acceleration of an electron in the first orbit of the hydrogen atom (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>) is :
solution:
For H-atom  n=1

mv2r=14πϵoe2r2  ...1

mvr=h2π  ...2

On squaring (2)

m2v2r2=h24π2

On dividing both sides by r3 ,

m2v2r=h24πr3 ⇒v2r=h24π2r3m2

Acceleration= h24π2r3m2.

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> moves in a circular orbit in a central potential field <math><mi>U</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If Bohr's quantization conditions are applied, radii of possible orbitals and energy levels vary with quantum number <math><mi>n</mi></math> as:
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> moves in a circular orbit in a central potential field <math><mi>U</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If Bohr's quantization conditions are applied, radii of possible orbitals and energy levels vary with quantum number <math><mi>n</mi></math> as:
solution:
Fr=-dUdr=-kr

For circular motion

|Fr|=kr=mv2r⇒kr2=mv2 ........(i)

Bohr's quantization ⇒mvr=nh2π ....(ii)

From (i) and (ii)

m2v2m=kr2

⇒1mnh2πr2=kr2⇒n2h24π2mk=r4

⇒r=h24π2mk1/4 n1/2

r∝n

From equation (i) U∝n

KE=12mv2; PE=12kr2

E=K+U=12mv2+12kr2=kr2∝n

subject: Physics
q:
A monochromatic light is incident on a hydrogen sample in ground state. Hydrogen atoms absorb a fraction of light and subsequently emit radiation of six different wavelengths. The frequency of incident light is x×1015 Hz. The value of x is ________
(Given h=4.25×10-15 eVs)
question:
A monochromatic light is incident on a hydrogen sample in ground state. Hydrogen atoms absorb a fraction of light and subsequently emit radiation of six different wavelengths. The frequency of incident light is x×1015 Hz. The value of x is ________
(Given h=4.25×10-15 eVs)
solution:
Let the higher state be n.

The number of wavelengths emitted is given by,

n(n-1)2=6⇒n2-n-12=0⇒n-4(n+3)=0⇒n=4

For hydrogen atom,
hν=13.611-116=13.6×1516eV⇒ν=Eh=13.6×15×1.6×10-1916×6.62×10-34=3×1015Hz

subject: Physics
q:
Radius of a certain orbit of hydrogen atom is $8.48 Ã…$. If energy of electron in this orbit is $E / x$. then $x=$ _____
(Given $\mathrm{a}_0=0.529 Ã…, E=$ energy of electron in ground state).
question:
Radius of a certain orbit of hydrogen atom is $8.48 Ã…$. If energy of electron in this orbit is $E / x$. then $x=$ _____
(Given $\mathrm{a}_0=0.529 Ã…, E=$ energy of electron in ground state).
solution:
We know $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{r}=0.529 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{Z}} \Rightarrow 8.48=0.529 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{1} \\ & \mathrm{n}^2=16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4 \end{aligned}$
We know $\mathrm{E} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}$ $\begin{aligned} & E_{n^{\text {th }}}=\frac{E}{16} \\ & x=16 \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A hydrogen atom changes its state from $n=3$ to $n=2$. Due to recoil, the percentage change in the wave length of emitted light is approximately $1 \times 10^{-n}$. The value of $n$ is_____. 
[Given $\mathrm{Rhc}=13.6 \mathrm{eV}, \mathrm{hc}=1242 \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{nm}, \mathrm{h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}$ mass of the hydrogenatom $=1.6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$ ]
question:
A hydrogen atom changes its state from $n=3$ to $n=2$. Due to recoil, the percentage change in the wave length of emitted light is approximately $1 \times 10^{-n}$. The value of $n$ is_____. 
[Given $\mathrm{Rhc}=13.6 \mathrm{eV}, \mathrm{hc}=1242 \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{nm}, \mathrm{h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}$ mass of the hydrogenatom $=1.6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$ ]
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{E}=13.6\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right)=1.9 \mathrm{eV} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda} \\ & \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\Delta \mathrm{E}} \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{t}} \\ & 0=-\mathrm{mv}+\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda^{\prime \prime}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \lambda^{\prime}} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv^{2 }}+\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda^{\prime \prime}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \lambda^{\prime}}\right)^2+\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda^{\prime}} \end{aligned}$
Now $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{E}=\frac{h^2}{2 \mathrm{~m} \lambda^{\prime^2}}+\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda^{\prime}} \\ & \lambda^{\prime 2} \Delta \mathrm{E}-\mathrm{hc} \lambda^{\prime}-\frac{\mathrm{h}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}=0 \\ & \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{hc} \pm \sqrt{\mathrm{h}^2 \mathrm{c}^2+\frac{4 \Delta \mathrm{Eh}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}}}{2 \Delta \mathrm{E}} \\ & \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{hc} \pm \mathrm{hc} \sqrt{1+\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{mc}}}}{2 \Delta \mathrm{E}} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=\frac{1+\left(1+\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{mc}^2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{2}=\frac{1+1+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{mc}^2}}{2} \\ & \frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=1+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}}{2 \mathrm{mc}^2} \\ & \frac{\lambda^{\prime}-\lambda}{\lambda}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}}{2 \mathrm{mc}^2}=\frac{1.9 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \times 9 \times 10^{15}}=10^{-9} \\ & \therefore \% \text { change } \approx 10^{-7} \end{aligned}$
Correct answer 7

q: A particular hydrogen - like ion emits the radiation of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> when it makes transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The frequency of radiation emitted in transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=3</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>,</mo></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> _____.
question: A particular hydrogen - like ion emits the radiation of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> when it makes transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The frequency of radiation emitted in transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=3</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>,</mo></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> _____.
solution:
For Hydrogen like atom, energy emitted is E=-13.6Z21ni2-1nf2

⇒E=C1nf2-1ni2

⇒hν=C1nf2-1ni2

Therefore, the  ratiov1v2=1nf2-1ni22-11nf2-1ni23-1

=11-1411-19=3489

=34×98

⇒v1v2=2732

⇒v2=3227v1=3227×3×1015 Hz=329×1015 Hz

Hence, x=32.

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of momentum of the photons of the $1^{st}$ and $2^{nd}$ line of Balmer series of Hydrogen atoms is $\alpha/\beta$. The possible values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are:-
question: The ratio of momentum of the photons of the $1^{st}$ and $2^{nd}$ line of Balmer series of Hydrogen atoms is $\alpha/\beta$. The possible values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are:-
solution:
The momentum of a photon is given by $p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$.

From Rydberg's formula, the wave number is $\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$.

For the first line of the Balmer series, the transition is from $n_2 = 3$ to $n_1 = 2$.

$p_1 = \dfrac{h}{\lambda_1} = hR \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) = hR \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9} \right) = \dfrac{5hR}{36}$

For the second line of the Balmer series, the transition is from $n_2 = 4$ to $n_1 = 2$.

$p_2 = \dfrac{h}{\lambda_2} = hR \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2} \right) = hR \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right) = \dfrac{3hR}{16}$

The ratio of the momenta is:

$\dfrac{p_1}{p_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{5hR}{36}}{\dfrac{3hR}{16}} = \dfrac{5}{36} \times \dfrac{16}{3} = \dfrac{20}{27}$

Comparing with $\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$, the possible values are $\alpha = 20$ and $\beta = 27$.

Answer: $20$ and $27$

q: A diatomic molecule is made of two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ which are separated by a distance $r$. If we calculate its rotational energy by applying Bohr's rule of angular momentum quantization, its energy will be given by ( $n$ is an integer)
question: A diatomic molecule is made of two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ which are separated by a distance $r$. If we calculate its rotational energy by applying Bohr's rule of angular momentum quantization, its energy will be given by ( $n$ is an integer)
solution:
$r_1=\frac{m_2 r}{m_1+m_2} ; r_2=\frac{m_1 r}{m_1+m_2}$
$\left(l_1+l_2\right) \omega=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}=nh$
$K . E=\frac{1}{2}\left(l_1+l_2\right) \omega^2=\frac{n^2 h^2\left(m_1+m_2\right)}{2 m_1 m_2 r^2}$

question: The frequency of revolution of the electron in Bohr's orbit varies with $n$, the principal quantum number as
solution: $\begin{aligned} & f=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}=\left(\frac{V_0}{n}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi r_0 n^2}\right) \\ & f=\frac{V_0}{2 \pi r_0}=\left(\frac{1}{n^3}\right)=f_0\left(\frac{1}{n^3}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Muon $\left(\mu^{-1}\right)$ is negatively charged $(|\mathrm{q}|=|\mathrm{e}|)$ with a mass $\mathrm{m}_\mu=200 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}$, where $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}$ is the mass of the electron and e is the electronic charge. If $\mu^{-1}$ is bound to a proton to form a hydrogen like atom, identify the correct statements
(A) Radius of the muonic orbit is 200 times smaller than that of the electron
(B) the speed of the $\mu^{-1}$ in the $n$th orbit is $\frac{1}{200}$ times that of the election in the nth orbit
(C) The lonization energy of muonic atom is 200 times more than that of an hydrogen atom
(D) The momentum of the muon in the nth orbit is 200 times more than that of the electron
question:
Muon $\left(\mu^{-1}\right)$ is negatively charged $(|\mathrm{q}|=|\mathrm{e}|)$ with a mass $\mathrm{m}_\mu=200 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}$, where $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}$ is the mass of the electron and e is the electronic charge. If $\mu^{-1}$ is bound to a proton to form a hydrogen like atom, identify the correct statements
(A) Radius of the muonic orbit is 200 times smaller than that of the electron
(B) the speed of the $\mu^{-1}$ in the $n$th orbit is $\frac{1}{200}$ times that of the election in the nth orbit
(C) The lonization energy of muonic atom is 200 times more than that of an hydrogen atom
(D) The momentum of the muon in the nth orbit is 200 times more than that of the electron
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { (A) Radius of muon }=\frac{\text { Radius of hydrogen }}{200} \\
&\text { Radius of } \mathrm{H} \text { atom }=r=\frac{\in_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2} \\
&\text { Radius of muon }=r_\mu=\frac{\in_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi \times 200 m e^2} \\
&r_\mu=\frac{r}{200}
\end{aligned}
$$
(B) Velocity relation given is wrong
(C) Ionization energy in $e^{-} \mathrm{H}$ atom
$$
E=\frac{+m e^4}{8 \epsilon_0^2 n^2 h^2}
$$

$$
E_\mu=\frac{200 m e^4}{8 \epsilon_0^2 n^2 h^2}=200 E
$$
(D) Momentum of $\mathrm{H}$-atom
$$
m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}
$$
Hence (A), (C), (D) are correct.

q: In the line spectra of hydrogen atom, difference between the largest and the shortest wavelengths of the Lyman series is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>305</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math>. The corresponding difference for the Paschan series in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math> is:________
question: In the line spectra of hydrogen atom, difference between the largest and the shortest wavelengths of the Lyman series is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>305</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math>. The corresponding difference for the Paschan series in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math> is:________
solution:
1λmin=R1=R ; n=∞ to 1

1λmax=R1−14=3R4 ; n=2 to 1

⇒ λmax−λmin=43R−1R

304=13R                                              ......a

Paschen : Yλ'min=R19

and Yλ'max=R19−116=7R16×9

λ'max−λ'min=16×97R−9R=817R       ...b

ba=x3×304=817

⇒ x=10553.14

q: Radiation coming from transitions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> of hydrogen atoms fall on <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> ions in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> states. The possible transition of helium ions as they absorb energy from the radiation is:
question: Radiation coming from transitions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> of hydrogen atoms fall on <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> ions in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> states. solution:
E=13.6111-122 eV  ...(1)
For Helium:
E=13.6221n12-1n22 eV ...(2)
From equation (1) and (2)
13.6 34=13.6 4 1n12-1n22
1n12-1n22=316

subject: Physics
q: In a hydrogen like atom, when an electron jumps from the M-shell to the L-shell, the wavelength of emitted radiation is $L$. If an electron jumps from $\mathrm{N}$ -shell to the $\mathrm{L}$ -shell, the wavelength of emitted radiation will be:
question: In a hydrogen like atom, when an electron jumps from the M-shell to the L-shell, the wavelength of emitted radiation is $L$. If an electron jumps from $\mathrm{N}$ -shell to the $\mathrm{L}$ -shell, the wavelength of emitted radiation will be:
solution:
When electron jumps from $\mathrm{M} \rightarrow \mathrm{L}$ shell

$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{K}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{K} \times 5}{36}$

When eletron jumps from $\mathrm{N} \rightarrow \mathrm{L}$ shell

$\frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}=K\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right)=\frac{K \times 3}{16}$

solving equation (i) and (ii) we get

$\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{20}{27} \lambda$

subject: Physics
q: In the Bohr model an electron moves in a circular orbit around the proton. Considering the orbiting electron to be a circular current loop, the magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $n^{\text {th }}$ excited state, is :
question: In the Bohr model an electron moves in a circular orbit around the proton. Considering the orbiting electron to be a circular current loop, the magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $n^{\text {th }}$ excited state, is :
solution:
Magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ excited state, i.e., $n^{\prime}=(n+1)$
As magnetic moment $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}^2\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
& i_n=e V_n=\frac{m z^2 e^5}{4 \varepsilon_0^2 n^3 h^3} \\
& r_n=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 k z m e^2}\left(k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\right)
\end{aligned}$
Solving we get magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom for $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ excited state
$M_{n^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{e}{2 m}\right) \frac{n h}{2 \pi}$

subject: Physics
q: Using Bohr's model, calculate the ratio of the magnetic fields generated due to the motion of the electrons in the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $4^{\text{th}}$ orbits of hydrogen atom _______.
question: Using Bohr's model, calculate the ratio of the magnetic fields generated due to the motion of the electrons in the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $4^{\text{th}}$ orbits of hydrogen atom _______.
solution:
The magnetic field at the center of a circular orbit is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2r}$.

The equivalent current due to the revolving electron is $I = \dfrac{e}{T} = \dfrac{ev}{2\pi r}$.

Substituting $I$ into the expression for $B$, we get $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 ev}{4\pi r^2}$.

According to Bohr's model for a hydrogen atom, the velocity of the electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit is $v \propto \dfrac{1}{n}$ and the radius of the orbit is $r \propto n^2$.

Therefore, the magnetic field $B$ depends on the principal quantum number $n$ as $B \propto \dfrac{1/n}{(n^2)^2} \Rightarrow B \propto \dfrac{1}{n^5}$.

The ratio of the magnetic fields in the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $4^{\text{th}}$ orbits is $\dfrac{B_2}{B_4} = \left(\dfrac{n_4}{n_2}\right)^5 = \left(\dfrac{4}{2}\right)^5 = 2^5 = 32$.

Answer: $32$

q:
An excited He+ ion emits two photons in succession, with wavelengths 108.5 nm and 30.4 nm in making a transition to the ground state. The quantum number n, corresponding to its initial excited state is



(for a photon of wavelength λ, energy E=1240 eVλ(in nm))
question:
An excited He+ ion emits two photons in succession, with wavelengths 108.5 nm and 30.4 nm in making a transition to the ground state. The quantum number n, corresponding to its initial excited state is



(for a photon of wavelength λ, energy E=1240 eVλ(in nm))
solution:
E1=1240λ1=1240108.5≈11.43 eV
E2=1240λ2=124030.4≈40.79 eV
ETotal=E1+E1=52.22 eV
52.22=13.6221-1n2
52.22=54.41-1n2
0.96=1-1n2
1n2=0.04
n2=10.04=1004=25
n=5

q: For hydrogen atom, λ1 and λ2 are the wavelengths corresponding to the transitions 1 and 2 respectively as shown in figure. The ratio of λ1 and λ2 is x32. The value of x is ______.
question: For hydrogen atom, λ1 and λ2 are the wavelengths corresponding to the transitions 1 and 2 respectively as shown in figure. The ratio of λ1 and λ2 is x32. The value of x is ______.
solution:
Wavelength corresponding to the photon emitted in transition n=3 to n=1 is

1λ1=R112-132=8R9   ....(1)

Wavelength corresponding to the photon emitted for transition in transition n=2 to n=1 is

1λ2=R112-122=3R4    ....(2)

λ1λ2=3R4×98R=2732

Hence, the value of x=27.

question: A hydrogen atom in ground state is given an energy of $10.2 \mathrm{eV}$. How many spectral lines will be emitted due to transition of electrons?
solution: Hydrogen will be in first excited state therefore it will emit one spectral line corresponding to transition b/w energy level 2 to 1

q: The ratio of wavelength of spectral lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi>β</mi></msub></math> in the Balmer series is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>20</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the wavelength of Hα-line in Balmer series is given by

hcλα=13.614-19   ...1

The formula to calculate the wavelength of Hβ-line in Balmer series is given by

hcλβ=13.614-116   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) 
⇒λαλβ=14-11614-19
=3664×125
=2720

q: As an electron makes a transition from an excited state to the ground state of a hydrogen-like atom/ion
question: As an electron makes a transition from an excited state to the ground state of a hydrogen-like atom/ion
options: kinetic energy and total energy decrease but potential energy increases
its kinetic energy increases but potential energy and total energy decrease
kinetic energy, potential energy and total energy decrease
kinetic energy decreases, potential energy increases but total energy remains same
solution:
U=-e24πε0r, U= potential energy, K=e24πε0r
K=kinetic energy. E=U+K=-e28πε0r, E=total energy∴ As electron de-excites from an excited state to ground state, K increases, but U and E decrease.

q: As per given figure A, B and C are the first, second and third excited energy levels of hydrogen atom respectively. If the ratio of the two wavelengthsi.e. λ1λ2 is 74n, then the value of n will be
question: As per given figure A, B and C are the first, second and third excited energy levels of hydrogen atom respectively. If the ratio of the two wavelengthsi.e. λ1λ2 is 74n, then the value of n will be
solution:
For λ1

1λ1=RZ2(122-132)=R536   ...(i)

For λ2,

1λ2=RZ2(132-142)=R7144   ...(ii)

Dividing (ii) by (i)

λ1λ2=7144536=720

So, 

74n=720⇒n=5
